seed_instruction: Write a email to your professor to ask for an extention on a project due in two days.
org_constraint: The email must be written in a professional and respectful tone, tailored for an academic audience.
new_constraint: Write the email in an informal tone, tailored for a casual audience.
instruction: Write a email to your professor to ask for an extention on a project due in two days. The email must be written in a professional and respectful tone, tailored for an academic audience. Write the email in an informal tone, tailored for a casual audience.
response:
thought

*   Task: Write two emails to a professor asking for a project extension (due in two days).
    *   Constraint 1: Professional and respectful tone (academic audience).
    *   Constraint 2: Informal tone (casual audience - *Wait, the prompt says "tailored for a casual audience" but it's still an email to a professor. Usually, "informal" in this context means a more relaxed but still polite relationship, or perhaps the prompt implies a different recipient? No, it says "Write an email to your professor... Write the email in an informal tone." This is a bit contradictory because professors usually require a baseline of professionalism. I will interpret "informal" as "friendly/conversational" while still acknowledging the professor-student dynamic.*)

    *   *Subject Line:* Clear and concise (Course Name, Project Name, Extension Request).
    *   *Salutation:* Formal (Dear Professor [Name]).
    *   *Context:* State the project and the current deadline.
    *   *Reason:* Provide a valid reason (illness, family emergency, heavy workload - keep it brief but honest).
    *   *Proposed New Deadline:* Be specific.
    *   *Closing:* Professional sign-off.

    *   *Subject Line:* Less formal (Quick question / Extension).
    *   *Salutation:* Friendly (Hi Professor [Name]).
    *   *Context:* Mention the project.
    *   *Reason:* More conversational.
    *   *Proposed New Deadline:* Flexible but clear.
    *   *Closing:* Casual sign-off (Best, Thanks).

    *   *Drafting Option 1 (Professional):*
        Subject: Extension Request: [Project Name] - [Your Name]
        Dear Professor [Last Name],
        I am writing to respectfully request an extension on the [Project Name] due this [Day of the week]. Due to [Reason: e.g., an unexpected personal emergency/illness], I am finding it difficult to complete the work to the standard I aim for by the current deadline. Would it be possible to submit the project by [New Date]? I appreciate your consideration. Sincerely, [Name].

    *   *Drafting Option 2 (Informal):*
        Subject: Question about the [Project Name] deadline
        Hi Professor [Last Name],
        I'm reaching out because I'm running a bit behind on the [Project Name] due in two days. [Reason: e.g., I've had a really hectic week/I'm feeling a bit under the weather]. Would you be open to me turning it in by [New Date] instead? Thanks so much for your help! Best, [Name].

    *   Check against constraints: Professional/Respectful? Yes. Informal/Casual? Yes.
    *   *Self-Correction during drafting:* For the "informal" one, I should make sure it doesn't sound *disrespectful*. Even an informal email to a professor should maintain a level of courtesy.Here are two ways to phrase this request, depending on your relationship with your professor.

### Option 1: Professional and Respectful
*Use this if you do not know the professor well, if the class is very large, or if the professor is known for being strict.*

**Subject:** Extension Request: [Project Name] – [Your Full Name]

Dear Professor [Last Name],

I hope this email finds you well.

I am writing to respectfully request an extension on the [Project Name] currently due on [Original Due Date]. Due to [briefly mention reason: e.g., an unexpected family emergency / a period of illness / a heavy overlap of major deadlines], I am concerned that I will not be able to complete the assignment to the best of my ability by the current deadline.

Would it be possible to submit my project by [Proposed New Date] instead? I am committed to producing high-quality work and would greatly appreciate the extra time to ensure the project meets your expectations.

Thank you for your time and for considering my request.

Best regards,

[Your Name]
[Your Student ID Number]

***

### Option 2: Informal and Casual
*Use this if you have a friendly rapport with the professor, if it is a small seminar, or if the professor has encouraged a more relaxed communication style.*

**Subject:** Question about the [Project Name] deadline

Hi Professor [Last Name],

I hope you’re having a good week.

I’m reaching out because I’m running a bit behind on the [Project Name] due this [Day of the week]. [Briefly mention reason: e.g., I’ve had a really hectic week / I’ve been feeling a bit under the weather], and I want to make sure I have enough time to really polish my final draft.

Would you be open to me turning it in by [Proposed New Date] instead? Please let me know if that works for you.

Thanks so much!

Best,

[Your Name]
